evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items

paragraphs.text: On the 30th of January 2024, the staff of the Gaming Commission organized a workshop about paid random items. During this event, it discussed the topic with representatives from different stakeholders. This document provides an overview of the major findings of the workshop.

evidence_quotes.quote: paid loot boxes were a form of games of chance.

paragraphs.text: In 2018, the Gaming Commission published a research report stating that paid loot boxes were a form of games of chance. Consequently, these practices are considered to fall under the scope of the Gambling Act of 7 may 1999, including its general ban on unlicensed games of chance. In the years that followed, this generalized ban proved difficult to enforce, and questions arose with regards to its proportionality when applied to paid random items. In 2022, the Gaming Commission published an advice calling for a broader dialogue about paid random items.

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: Regulators from other jurisdictions have taken a similar interest in the topic, and a recent resolution from the Parliament of the European Union has called on the European Commission to examine the topic of loot boxes. Furthermore, the European Commission updated Guidance to the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive from December 2021 includes measures on paid loot boxes.
On the 30th of January 2024, the Gaming Commission organized a workshop on the topic. During this event, it discussed the topic with representatives from different stakeholders.

evidence_quotes.quote: regulation of paid random items.

paragraphs.text: The event was aimed at reaching answers to the following questions regarding the regulation of paid random items.
Why?
What are potential risks for players?
Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?
| What? | - Should it be possible to offer these transactions legally?
Should there be specific measures to protect players, and if so, which ones?
What are the most adequate terms and definitions to use in this context?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: How to address differences and similarities between various common practices (e.g. loot boxes, trading card games, surprise packs, etc.)?
Which measures does the industry already implement, to address concerns of player protection? | | --- | --- | | How? Where? | - What is the desired legal framework?
How should these measures be divided between national gambling regulation, European consumer law, and tools of self-regulation?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes and similar mechanisms are legal in most European jurisdictions?

paragraphs.text: How to address differences between jurisdictions, given that loot boxes and similar mechanisms are legal in most European jurisdictions? |

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items

paragraphs.text: Different organizations participated in the event, each with their own connection to the topic of paid random items.
Report – Workshop 30/01/2024
Belgian Gaming Commission

A draft report on the workshop was drawn up by the staff of the Belgian Gaming Commission, who organized the event, and sent to the different participating organisations. Is was subsequently amended to reflect any relevant comments, without adding to or detracting from the content of the discussions and guidelines that emerged during the day.

The content of this report was presented to the Gaming Commission during its session of 21 November 2024. It should be noted that this document has not been submitted to each participating organisation for approval. Neither does it constitute a decision from the Gaming Commission.
At the start of the event, the participating organizations gave a brief introduction of themselves and their connection with the subject matter.

Participants were then split up into different groups, making sure that each group was composed of members from different categories of organizations. Each group also had a rapporteur, acting as the single point of contact within the group. During the discussion phase, groups were given different time slots to discuss the research questions.

At the end of the event, each rapporteur presented the main findings of their group. These conclusions were discussed between the different groups, and noted by the representatives from the Gaming Commission.
The working groups identified the following possible risks for players.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: **Permanent Accessibility** – Traditionally, randomized transactions used to be less accessible to players due to their physical nature. Trading cards, for example, were mainly purchased in physical establishments, each with their own opening hours and real-world distance from consumers. Both classical and more recent forms of randomized transactions, across all sectors, are increasingly available online, where these physical limitations do not exist. Furthermore, digital goods are usually not limited in stock, and accessible to players at all times.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: **Young audience** – A substantial part of the audience for randomized transactions, consists of young people. Not only people under the age of 18, but also young adults, are not fully mature and may be less experienced. Children in particular tend to have an increased vulnerability in terms of playing behaviour and may therefore need more supervision and safeguards in their digital activities. Furthermore, older players typically have more spending power to absorb negative financial effects.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Paid random items in their digital form tend to have a high event frequency
Persons struggling with impulse control, may be more prone to this risk.

paragraphs.text: **Event frequency** – Paid random items in their digital form tend to have a high event frequency, meaning that there is little time between accessing a random item, having the result revealed, and accessing a new random item. The shorter the amount of time between openings, the more dangerous a transaction can become. Persons struggling with impulse control, may be more prone to this risk.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Intermittent rewards such as these are one of the driving mechanisms that also make gambling activities more addictive.

paragraphs.text: **Intermittent rewards** – When players purchase a paid random item, they always receive a reward. This is one of the elements that differentiates these transactions from real-world gambling. That said, at the time of committing to a randomized transaction, the player does not know what the result will be. This can be either a win (desirable items) or a loss (low-value items). Intermittent rewards such as these are one of the driving mechanisms that also make gambling activities more addictive.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game items

paragraphs.text: **Value of in-game items** – This variance in possible rewards may be difficult to assess objectively, because part of the value that in-game items provide, may be cosmetic or otherwise subjective to players. Unlike what is the case for gambling, there is not always a financial aspect, as the random item cannot be cashed out.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Video games have become a very social activity, and in-game items can hold special value to members of online communities.

paragraphs.text: **Social risks** – Video games have become a very social activity, and in-game items can hold special value to members of online communities. Skins and other cosmetic items are often a form of self-expression, or an indication of status. Players may experience social pressure and a fear of missing out ('FOMO'). While only a small amount of gamers experience these issues, these players may be extra sensitive to gambling-like elements of a transaction. That said, peer influence and the sense of community can have positive effects on players as well.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: open loot boxes or booster packs for entertainment purposes.
It is not possible to exclude cases where content creators are paid to show only the good results that they get.

paragraphs.text: **Used for secondary entertainment** – Randomized transactions may also be a source of entertainment for persons that are not directly engaging with them. For example, influencers, streamers or other content creators, which act independently from video game developers and publishers, may open loot boxes or booster packs for entertainment purposes. It is not possible to exclude cases where content creators are paid to show only the good results that they get. This content does not always provide a fair image to players.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: desirable items are made available only through randomized mechanisms
In that case, players may be tempted to keep on spending in order to get a rare item they want.

paragraphs.text: Exclusive availability - Players may be incentivized even further, if desirable items are made available only through randomized mechanisms, and not through individual purchases or gameplay performance. In that case, players may be tempted to keep on spending in order to get a rare item they want.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes in a video game may contain usable items that influence gameplay and provide various advantages to the player that owns or equips them.
These are sometimes referred to as pay-to-win mechanics.

paragraphs.text: Competitive advantages and pay-to-win mechanics – While rewards from paid random items are often cosmetic in nature, there are also cases where they provide a competitive advantage to players. For example, in a trading card game, a booster pack may contain mostly regular cards, but also have a slight chance to contain a more powerful card. As another example, loot boxes in a video game may contain usable items that influence gameplay and provide various advantages to the player that owns or equips them. Usually, players are able to obtain rewards through their play time and gameplay achievements alone. However, mechanisms through which players who invest extra money in a game have a competitive advantage over players who do not, also exist. These are sometimes referred to as pay-to-win mechanics. They can tempt players to purchase even more random items. Players may see these transactions as an investment into their own performance.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: The presentation of loot boxes sometimes uses certain design elements (e.g. flashing lights, vivid animations, spinning wheels, and appealing sounds) that are similar to those present in games of chance, and that may cause rises in dopamine and adrenaline levels in players.
loot boxes

paragraphs.text: Presentation - The presentation of loot boxes sometimes uses certain design elements (e.g. flashing lights, vivid animations, spinning wheels, and appealing sounds) that are similar to those present in games of chance, and that may cause rises in dopamine and adrenaline levels in players. Moreover, these design aesthetics are often perceived as cute, innocent and/or familiar, which may pause barriers for a thoughtful engagement with these elements.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: gambling-like elements in games
randomized transactions

paragraphs.text: Gateway hypothesis – Analyses conducted on the longitudinal data from the Gam(e)(a)ble project indicate that more frequent engagement in randomized transactions predicts increased participation in gambling activities at a later stage. These longitudinal associations, which remain significant even when controlling for variables such as gender, age, and temperament, are consistent with a few other longitudinal studies. Future research would do well to control for additional possible confounding variables. The longitudinal findings currently available point to a clear directional path from engagement in gambling-like elements in games to actual gambling.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: The odds of winning a certain item may be unknown or not clearly displayed.
The use of general categories of rarity has a risk of misleading players, because it is possible that individual items have their own separate rarity within these categories.

paragraphs.text: Lack of transparency – Players often have limited precontractual information at their disposal. For example, the odds of winning a certain item may be unknown or not clearly displayed. The use of general categories of rarity has a risk of misleading players, because it is possible that individual items have their own separate rarity within these categories.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Afterwards, it may become progressively more difficult to obtain the same reward without spending (more) money.

paragraphs.text: Inconsistent reward mechanisms - Another existing strategy, is to make it easier or more common for players to obtain valuable rewards when they initially start playing. Afterwards, it may become progressively more difficult to obtain the same reward without spending (more) money.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: In-game currencies – It is a common practice that video games make use of in-game currencies.

paragraphs.text: In-game currencies – It is a common practice that video games make use of in-game currencies. These tokens may be different to handle than actual money would. Furthermore, players need to mentally convert the in-game currency to actual monetary currency. For these reasons, it should be examined whether the use of an in-game currency may confuse players, or have effects that are similar to the use of casino chips.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: randomized transactions usually require a financial commitment from the player.

paragraphs.text: Financial risks – The elements above may lead to players overspending, as randomized transactions usually require a financial commitment from the player. This is also the case for minors, even though parents should theoretically be in charge of their spendings.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: The working groups raised the issues below, without adopting a definitive position on them. Either because the subject fell outside the scope of the workshop, or because further research is needed to gain more insight.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Simulated gambling – In some environments, players are able to participate in lifelike simulations of gambling, typically without being able to cash out their winnings in the direct gaming environment.

paragraphs.text: Simulated gambling – In some environments, players are able to participate in lifelike simulations of gambling, typically without being able to cash out their winnings in the direct gaming environment. These games may give rise to false ideas about actual gambling products. For example, social casino games can create warped perceptions, causing players to expect to win more than they would in actual games of chance. The many similarities to classical games of chance and the increased prevalence of simulated gambling, have the effect of normalizing monetary gambling. It is possible that engaging in simulated gambling leads to actual gambling. Within the PEGI system, social casinos are always classified as PEGI 18. The same is true for content that may teach or encourage real-life gambling.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Randomized transactions pose an additional risk if they are used in combination with aggressive or misleading commercial practices, which are a threat for adults and minors alike.

paragraphs.text: Advertising and other commercial practices – Randomized transactions pose an additional risk if they are used in combination with aggressive or misleading commercial practices, which are a threat for adults and minors alike. The same is true for aggressive forms of advertising and publicity. It is important that all players can make informed decisions when interacting with a game, and the broader gaming ecology. Advertising should always be conducted in a responsible way.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Tradability – In some economies, players can trade the items they have obtained through randomized transactions.

paragraphs.text: Tradability – In some economies, players can trade the items they have obtained through randomized transactions. The working groups did not reach a conclusion as to whether this element has an influence on the risk for players, and whether this influence is positive or negative.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: free-to-play models pose risks for players.

paragraphs.text: Free-to-play models – It should be examined whether free-to-play models pose risks for players. Competition within the industry may force companies to seek out more risky business models, that may not always prioritize the best interest of players. Regardless of the business model, there is always a price that players pay directly or indirectly.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: skin gambling

paragraphs.text: Fraud and safety – It is also worth looking into other risks of paid random items that are not directly linked to player protection. For example: tax evasion and money laundering (in the case of illegal activities such as skin gambling).

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Reaching a larger and more diverse group of players

paragraphs.text: Reaching a larger and more diverse group of players – When it comes to awareness raising efforts, some players and parents are more difficult to reach than others. All actors that intervene in regulation, should be mindful of this and strive to reach an audience that is as broad and diverse as possible.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: in the realm of gaming

paragraphs.text: Insufficient research – More generally, additional research is needed that investigates the effects of randomized transactions and contemporary business models that emerged in the realm of gaming. Since 2018, the industry conducts annual studies on the in-game spending. In the future, further collaboration between researchers and the videogames industry would be useful. This would require, amongst others, mechanisms for researchers to request data to conduct research. For example: data on how many loot boxes the average player buys, or which groups spend more and on which transactions.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: What are the most adequate terms and definitions to use in this context?
How to address differences and similarities between various common practices?

paragraphs.text: 5.2. What are the most adequate terms and definitions to use in this context? How to address differences and similarities between various common practices?

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: General descriptions – Definitions used in legal texts should be general and clearly set out which frameworks apply to what. Rather than referring to set-in-stone concepts such as loot boxes or trading card games, more neutral terms should be used. Regulations should focus on elements of a product that may cause risks. The scope of both consumer protection and self-regulation should evolve along with business models used by the industry.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: Notion of ‘paid random items’ - During the discussions of the working groups, the term ‘paid random items’ was brought up as a general descriptor for the subject matter of the workshop. Without singling out a single type of transaction, this notion includes many common practices like loot boxes, booster packs in trading card games, or surprise packs. Some of these transactions pertain to physical goods, while others are entirely digital.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items
addiction or problematic playing behaviour

paragraphs.text: Public health perspective - Any regulations that apply to paid random items, should maintain references to the mental health and wellbeing effects of these transactions. More specifically, it should be avoided that paid random items cause addiction or problematic playing behaviour. The contents of any regulations on the topic should be based not only on the inherent fairness of transactions, but also on the potential risks in terms of public health and wellbeing.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes

paragraphs.text: Typology of randomized transactions - The European Parliament has published a list with different types of loot boxes that exist. Similarly, contributors to the Gam(e)(a)ble project have published a paper that describes how gambling(-like) elements structurally manifest themselves in the design of contemporary games. The results of these typologies should be examined. It is important to have a future-proof classification system, in which new practices can fit as well.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: 5.3. Should it be possible to offer these transactions legally?

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: a general ban on paid random items is not desirable.

paragraphs.text: Generalized ban is not desirable – The working groups were in agreement that a general ban on paid random items is not desirable. This approach would cause many practical difficulties, and would be disproportionate towards the industry and its consumers. A better approach would be to allow paid random items, while still preventing and controlling for the aforementioned possible risks. In any case, products should respect all applicable legislation, including gambling law at the national level and consumer protection law, data protection and platform regulation at the EU level.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: 5.4. Should there be specific measures to protect players, and if so, which ones?

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: The working groups have suggested the following measures to address the concerns and potential risks mentioned above.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes: Breaking the loop – To prevent players spending excessive amounts of time and money, mechanisms should exist that break or temporally pause the flow between considering and committing to a

paragraphs.text: Breaking the loop – To prevent players spending excessive amounts of time and money, mechanisms should exist that break or temporally pause the flow between considering and committing to a

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items that are opened digitally, could introduce or increase delays between different openings.
In video games, transactions are and should remain separate from gameplay.

paragraphs.text: purchase, playing, knowing and understanding the result, and playing again. For example, paid random items that are opened digitally, could introduce or increase delays between different openings. In video games, transactions are and should remain separate from gameplay. This ensures that the commercial intent behind in-game purchases, including the cost of such in-game purchases in real-world money, is clearly and unambiguously distinguished from gameplay so that it is obvious to players when they are being asked to make a financial decision.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: **Transparency** - Players should always be able to make an informed choice, and the rules of the transaction should be clear to them from the start.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: odds of winning
real-world cost of items

paragraphs.text: For paid random items: probabilities and prices – Products that offer paid random items, could increase transparency when it comes to the odds of winning, and the cost of in-game transactions. For example, games could increase the visibility of the odds players have to win certain items, and could display them in-game or in the same environment where the transaction occurs. Games could also inform players of the real-world cost of items, which may be obscured if in-game currencies are used. Information for players should include ‘honest odds’, meaning that listings of probabilities are clear, transparent and fair to all players, correct and exhaustive, rather than using generalized categories of rarity.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: the inclusion of paid random items.

paragraphs.text: In general: content of games – More generally, players are not always aware beforehand of the content they may experience when playing a game. While a certain element of surprise is inherent to the medium, it could be useful if developers explain the content of the game in brief videos. For example, informing consumers about the gameplay elements, the presence of violence, or the inclusion of paid random items. It should be noted that large steps have already been taken in this regard through the PEGI classification system.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: the videogames industry has already taken steps to remove such unnecessary references to gambling from their products.

paragraphs.text: **Reducing gambling references** – Products should not include unnecessary (or ‘lazy’) references to gambling. For example, they should not use imagery similar to casino games, merely because they contain randomized elements. It should be noted that the videogames industry has already taken steps to remove such unnecessary references to gambling from their products.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Players should have an active choice on whether to use randomized transactions.
For example, some games allow players to obtain content merely through the rewards they receive by spending time in the game.
Similar effects could be achieved by allowing players to craft or convert items they do not want, into items they do want.
The same is true for systems where players can trade in-game items with each other, although it is unclear whether the overall effects of such trading systems are positive or negative.

paragraphs.text: **Alternatives and player choice** - Players should have an active choice on whether to use randomized transactions. Ideally, items should be available through other means than randomized transactions as well. Players may be less compelled to buy random items if alternatives are available. For example, some games allow players to obtain content merely through the rewards they receive by spending time in the game. Similar effects could be achieved by allowing players to craft or convert items they do not want, into items they do want. The same is true for systems where players can trade in-game items with each other, although it is unclear whether the overall effects of such trading systems are positive or negative.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: The industry and policy makers should consider the effects of having a market where items obtained from randomized transactions can be traded and cashed out (open-loop systems).

paragraphs.text: **Tradability** – The industry and policy makers should consider the effects of having a market where items obtained from randomized transactions can be traded and cashed out (open-loop systems). The alternative is a system where all items remain within the (digital) environment of the game itself (closed-loop systems).

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: tradability may increase the actual or perceived value of in-game items
secondary markets, that are not authorized or controlled by the game publishers
skin gambling, a practice where players can use in-game items as a wager in traditional games

paragraphs.text: On one hand, tradability may increase the actual or perceived value of in-game items, which may in turn incentivize players to play more. Furthermore, tradability may give rise to secondary markets, that are not authorized or controlled by the game publishers. For instance, derived games have arisen on unauthorized third-party websites. An infamous example is skin gambling, a practice where players can use in-game items as a wager in traditional games of

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: games of chance

paragraphs.text: chance. These products are already considered games of chance in some jurisdictions, and should be further looked into by regulators.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: prohibits players from using in-game content to engage in unlawful gambling or betting activities (so-called skin gambling or skin betting)

paragraphs.text: Signatories to the PEGI Code of Conduct must have a policy that explicitly prohibits players from using in-game content to engage in unlawful gambling or betting activities (so-called skin gambling or skin betting). These policies should include clear wording on the enforcement of potential penalties such as suspending or banning players in the case of illegal trading. The members of Video Games Europe (VGE) and the European Games Developer Federation (EGDF) welcome additional assistance from regulators in combating such unauthorised uses of in-game content.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Having the possibility to purchase items directly drives the price of individual items down, and provides players an alternative to randomized transactions.

paragraphs.text: On the other hand, having an open marketplace can be to the advantage of players. Having the possibility to purchase items directly drives the price of individual items down, and provides players an alternative to randomized transactions.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: a randomized transaction to be classified as a game of chance.

paragraphs.text: In some jurisdictions, the tradability of items already has a legal significance. A trading system causes items to have monetary value, which may in turn cause a randomized transaction to be classified as a game of chance.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: The working groups did not reach a conclusion as to whether regulation should encourage or discourage tradability.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: in-game transactions.

paragraphs.text: **Spending limits** – Players and parents should be able to decide beforehand how much they want to spend on in-game transactions. It should be noted that for minors, mechanisms to do so already exist within parental control tools.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: time restrictions could be an efficient measure to counter the otherwise consistent availability of transactions where the entire process (purchase and opening) takes place online.

paragraphs.text: **Time restrictions** – In the same vein, time restrictions could be an efficient measure to counter the otherwise consistent availability of transactions where the entire process (purchase and opening) takes place online.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: AI profiling

paragraphs.text: **Age verification** - Young people are particularly vulnerable to the potential dangers of randomized transactions. Any regulation regarding paid random items should provide tailored attention to these groups. More thorough age verification measures are needed provided they do not create more or new risks in terms of data reliability, data privacy, data security, and data inclusivity/accessibility and impacts negatively the rights of the child. That way, platforms know which users require parental consent or are subject to other rules before engaging in randomized transactions. What the age thresholds should be for these specific measures (e.g. 18 or 25 years old), remains a topic for discussion. Which age assurance methods are superior, is also not entirely clear. On one hand, there are age estimation methods such as AI profiling, biometric analysis, capacity testing, facial age estimations. On the other hand, there are age verification methods based on official ID document, ID check and photo, digital identities (eID), and proxies of ID documents. All of these methods have their own advantages and disadvantages.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: **Effects to be measured** - It should be noted that the effectiveness of the measures mentioned above must be monitored and assessed, especially those that have not been applied to paid random items yet.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: 5.5. Which measures does the industry already implement, to address concerns of player protection?

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: It should be noted that the industry already implements various measures to address concerns of player protection. Some examples were brought up during the discussions, and are listed below.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: PEGI Code of Conduct – The PEGI System is based on a *Code of Conduct*, consisting of a set of rules to which every publisher using the PEGI system is contractually committed. The Code deals with age labelling, promotion and marketing and reflects the video games industry’s commitment to provide information to the public in a responsible manner.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: (in-game purchases and paid random items descriptors)
disclose probabilities,
provide transparency about the cost of in-game items,
policy against skin gambling.

paragraphs.text: In-game monetization - The PEGI Code of Conduct sets a few basic standards to ensure in-game monetisation happens responsibly and in compliance with all laws and regulations. This includes requirements to display information (in-game purchases and paid random items descriptors), disclose probabilities, provide transparency about the cost of in-game items, and to have policy against skin gambling.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: Transparent and Fair Purchases of In-Game Content

paragraphs.text: VGE-EGDF Commitments – Video Games Europe (VGE) and the European Games Developer Federation (EGDF) have published a set of *commitments in terms of Transparent and Fair Purchases of In-Game Content*. These include the updated PEGI Code of Conduct, and add five additional measures:

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: tools to manage, limit or block purchases so that children do not engage in any financial transactions without the consent of their parents or guardians;

evidence_quotes.quote: transactions must be kept separate from gameplay so that the commercial intent behind in-game purchases is clearly and unambiguously distinguished from gameplay so that it is obvious to players when they are being asked to make a financial decision;

paragraphs.text: transactions must be kept separate from gameplay so that the commercial intent behind in-game purchases is clearly and unambiguously distinguished from gameplay so that it is obvious to players when they are being asked to make a financial decision;

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: member companies provide fair and reasonable refund policies and instructions on how to turn on and manage parental tools and family settings;

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: players are informed sufficiently in advance of the discontinuation of a purchasable in-game currency;

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: using in-game content to engage in unauthorised trading.

paragraphs.text: member companies must have policies in place that explicitly prohibit players from using in-game content to engage in unauthorised trading.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: unnecessary references to gambling in videogames.

paragraphs.text: Reducing gambling references – The videogames industry has already taken steps to reduce unnecessary references to gambling in videogames. Within the PEGI system, social casino games are

evidence_quotes.quote: interactive games throughout Europe.

paragraphs.text: The system is supported by the major console manufacturers, including Sony, Microsoft and Nintendo, as well as by publishers and developers of interactive games throughout Europe. The age rating system was developed by the Interactive Software Federation of Europe (now Video Games Europe).

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: The day-to-day management, supervision and development of the PEGI system was handed to PEGI s.a., an independent, not-for-profit company with a social purpose established under Belgian law. Drawing on wide experience, PEGI is steered by way of a number of boards and committees as envisaged in article 12 of the PEGI Code of Conduct.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: always classified as PEGI 18. The same is true for content that may teach or encourage real-life gambling.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: monitoring and potentially controlling whether and how children spend money in games, including random items.

paragraphs.text: **Parental tools** - Parental control tools are already in place on all major platforms and are actively promoted to assist parents in monitoring and potentially controlling whether and how children spend money in games, including random items. Most parental control tools are based on the parents first setting up a parent account, followed by the child account, to minimize any data collection of the child. The parent will then be invited to set up the 4 most common types of functionalities: spending, playtime, interactions with others, and access to games according to age label.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

paragraphs.text: There is still room to strengthen and improve these tools, and the information to parents about them. Additionally, there is a need to better understand how they can be embedded in a broader approach to parental mediation and parent-child interactions. Participants in the working groups suggested that protection measures should be enabled by default, which is currently not the case for most existing parental control tools.

section_path: 5.1. What are potential risks for players? Which elements of the game/transaction pose particular risks to players?

evidence_quotes.quote: How to address differences between jurisdictions, given that loot boxes and similar mechanisms are legal in most European jurisdictions?

paragraphs.text: As part of the general question “What is the desired legal framework?”, working groups were also asked to answer the following questions: “How should these measures be divided between national gambling regulation, European consumer law, and tools of self-regulation?” and “How to address differences between jurisdictions, given that loot boxes and similar mechanisms are legal in most European jurisdictions?”. As these questions are linked to each other, the feedback from the working groups has been combined and summarized below.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: There is a need for a clear legal framework when it comes to paid random items.

paragraphs.text: **Need for clarity** – There is a need for a clear legal framework when it comes to paid random items. The existence of grey areas is detrimental towards legal certainty for both players and the industry.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items

paragraphs.text: **Different possible systems** – Any regulation of paid random items, should take into account the advantages and disadvantages of the frameworks that exist currently. The working groups have summarized these strengths and weaknesses as follows:

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: | | Advantages | Disadvantages | | --- | --- | --- | | National (gambling) law | - Attention to public health aspects.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: gambling regulation (e.g. spending limits)

paragraphs.text: Experience from what has already been done in gambling regulation (e.g. spending limits). | - Contents are different in every jurisdiction and difficult to apply cross-border.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Uses different definitions compared to other legal systems.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: gambling products

paragraphs.text: Not intended to be applied to paid random items. These products are of a different nature from gambling products, and exist in a different environment.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Gambling regulators in their current form are not equipped to take on this subject matter as an added responsibility. | | European Union (consumer protection) law | - Instruments of European Union law can provide a harmonized set of rules.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Case law from the European Court of Justice exists and is in constant development. This case law is strengthened with input from all member states, and can help clear out grey areas.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Rules regarding randomized transactions can be encompassed within the more general framework of consumer protection.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: e.g. battle passes and progressive reward tracks
paid random items

paragraphs.text: EU consumer protection law is future proof and can take new practices (e.g. battle passes and progressive reward tracks) into account relatively easily. | - Consumer law can be quite general and difficult to apply to individual circumstances and products. There is a need for a more specific application of EU law to paid random items.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Not clear if there are enough methods of enforcement.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: The videogames sector is very aware of what is going on in the industry, and can react quickly to new developments.

paragraphs.text: Creating this regulation takes a longer time. | | Self-regulation | - The videogames sector is very aware of what is going on in the industry, and can react quickly to new developments. This may address the longer time it takes in other legal systems to adopt legislation.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: loot boxes in videogames.

paragraphs.text: Can translate broader regulation to specific situations. For example: loot boxes in videogames. | - Enforcement is set up by the industry itself.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Current content descriptors need to become more descriptive.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: It should be evaluated whether self-regulation, when used as a general framework, would provide the same level of protection as an external legal framework would. |

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items.

paragraphs.text: Matter to be regulated mainly by EU Consumer law – There was a consensus between the working groups that given the advantages and shortcomings mentioned above, European Union law, and in particular consumer protection law, is the most appropriate framework to regulate paid random items.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: Layered approach – Ideally, paid random items should be subject to a form of co-regulation consisting of three main layers.

paragraphs.text: Layered approach – Ideally, paid random items should be subject to a form of co-regulation consisting of three main layers.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items.

paragraphs.text: Gambling Law (divider) – National gambling legislation should have a limited dividing role when it comes to paid random items. It can determine which actors are competent for which practices. It can be the first layer in a broader legal framework, determining general requirements for which practices do and do not fall under its scope. In other words, national laws can draw lines which regular products should not cross, in order not to risk being considered a gambling product.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: They can define measures to protect players, and to prevent harmful elements of gambling products from bleeding over into paid random items.
gambling products
paid random items

paragraphs.text: European Union Law (substance) - Within these confines, consumer law should take over and make sure everything happens correctly. Consumer law, and by extension the laws of the European Union in general (e.g. advertising, data protection), should constitute the second layer in the regulatory framework. They can define measures to protect players, and to prevent harmful elements of gambling products from bleeding over into paid random items. Most of the measures suggested in the previous section, should be implemented in this second layer.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: Self-regulation constitutes the final layer, and can tailor the general provisions of European legislation into specific standards that can be monitored and enforced.
Parts of the industry that require specific measures, can be addressed in this way.

paragraphs.text: Self-regulation (implementation) - Self-regulation constitutes the final layer, and can tailor the general provisions of European legislation into specific standards that can be monitored and enforced. Parts of the industry that require specific measures, can be addressed in this way. Self-regulation can also address short-term needs while other regulations are still production.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: addressing paid random items.

paragraphs.text: These layers have a shared responsibility in addressing paid random items. If it is determined that transaction constitutes gambling, national legislation has the tools to address it (e.g. licensing requirements and age verification). If not, the consumer protection law is there to ensure certain standards for player protection. Self-regulation can make sure that the policy goals of the other two systems are achieved in specific situations.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: randomized purchases

paragraphs.text: Responsibility of parents - Aside from this regulatory approach, parents also have an important role to play when protecting young people from possible harms of randomized purchases and ensuring they can benefit from the opportunities that digital play can provide. They are advised and expected to keep an eye on the gaming activities of their children, to make sure all happens in a healthy way and context. That said, parents do not always participate in the gaming activities themselves.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: The industry has already taken valuable initiatives to support parents in guiding their children’s gaming behaviour.

paragraphs.text: The industry has already taken valuable initiatives to support parents in guiding their children’s gaming behaviour. For example, through parental tools available on all major platforms, the industry’s self-regulation with measures such as PEGI, informational websites for parents and the implementation of the industry’s Code of Conduct. That said, more measures to educate and inform parents are necessary as they navigate the digital landscape for their children.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

paragraphs.text: Just as the industry and regulators, parents have a role to play, and should be involved in a meaningful and effective way. While it is not possible to provide the exact same level of protection for every child, efforts should be made to reach as large and as diverse a group of parents as possible. Parents and society at large should not demonize games or similar products, and should instead work towards a balanced environment.

section_path: 5.6. What is the desired legal framework?

evidence_quotes.quote: paid random items

paragraphs.text: In conclusion, the working groups have noted several possible risks that paid random items may entail. While a ban on paid random items is not desirable, some measures should be put in place to ensure that players have a safe experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: a comprehensive framework for paid random items.

paragraphs.text: If regulation is to be adopted, this should ideally be done in a layered approach. National gambling legislation can have a dividing role and set out which transactions fall under its scope and which ones do not. That said, its specific subject matter and lack of harmonization prevent it from providing a comprehensive framework for paid random items.

The law of the European Union, in particular consumer protection law, seems to be the most appropriate instrument to implement the substance of these rules. Lastly, tools of self-regulation can tailor this framework to the needs of specific products and industries.
2 December 2024
Jonathan Van Damme
Event rapporteur
